title: A. Hejazi Corp. v Zoe et al [1995] LRSC 31; 38 LLR 61 (1995) (27 July 1995)
text:
A. Hejazi Corp. v Zoe et al [1995] LRSC 31; 38 LLR 61 (1995) (27 July 1995)
A. HEJAZI CORPORATION
, by and thru its President,
ANNIS HEJAZI, Plaintiff-In-Error, v.
HIS HONOUR C. ALEXANDER B. ZOE
,
Assigned Circuit Judge, Sixth Judicial Circuit, and
THE LIBERIAN-AMERICAN
INSURANCE CORPORATION
, by and thru its Resident Agent, COUNSELLOR OSWALD N.
TWEH, Defendants-In-Error.
PETITION FOR WRIT OF ERROR TO THE CIRCUIT COURT FOR THE SIXTH
JUDICIAL CIRCUIT, MONTSERRADO COUNTY.
Heard: April 17, 1995. Decided: July 27, 1995.
1. Sickness of a counsel for a party or his material witness is good ground
for postponement or continuance of a case, but the court
must be timely notified
by a motion for continuance with notice to the opposite party, and such motion
must be heard in open court
with the movant present to argue.
2. Where there is more than one lawyer in a case, the sickness of one lawyer
will not excuse the other who is available and has signed
the notice of
assignment from appearing.
3. A writ of error is a writ by which the Supreme Court calls up for review a
judgment of an inferior court from which an appeal was
not announced on
rendition of judgment.
4. A party against whom judgment has been rendered, who has for "good reason"
failed to make timely announcement of the taking of
an appeal from such
judgment, may, within six (6) months after its rendition, file with the Clerk of
the Supreme Court, an application
for leave for a review by the Supreme Court
upon a writ of error.
5. "Good reason", for the purpose of a writ of error, means those
circumstances under which a party was not afforded the opportunity
to have his
day in court due to the failure of the court to notify him of the time and place
of trial, or of the rendition of judgment,
and therefore denied opportunity to
except and announce an appeal from the judgment.
6. The "good reason" allegation is not conclusive; the plaintiff-in-error
must establish, and the court must be convinced, that the
reason alleged is good
in law.
7. A writ of error may be granted when an inferior tribunal has denied to a
litigant his day in court, where the appellant has lost
his right of statutory
appeal without laches, or where he is incompetent to act, or not notified of the
rendition of the decision
until the statutory time for the taking of an appeal
has elapsed.
8. "A day in court" is the time appointed for one whose rights are called
judiciously in question, or liable to be affected by judicial
action, to appear
in court and be heard in his own behalf.
9. "A day in court", as generally used, means not so much the time appointed
for a hearing, as the opportunity to present one's claims
or rights in a proper
forensic hearing before a competent tribunal. A litigant has his "day in court"
when he has been duly cited
to appear and has been afforded the opportunity to
appear and be heard.
10. If an action is timely commenced and is terminated in any manner other
than by a dismissal of the complaint for failure to prosecute
the action or a
final judgment on the merits, the plaintiff may commence a new action upon the
same right to relief after the expiration
of the time limited by statute
therefor and within six (6) months after the termination, and the defendant may
interpose any defence
or counter claim which might have been interposed in the
original action.
11. To reserve ruling on a motion at the close of argument or to rule
immediately thereafter, is left with the discretion of the trial
judge and his
legal ability to pass on the issues raised. Ruling on a motion immediately after
it is heard does not constitute reversible
error.
12. Every man is entitled to take full advantage of the law in defense of his
right; but if he fails to do so, the law gives no protection
to him for abusing
his own rights.
13. He who is silent when he should speak, assents.
14. The unreasonable delay of a party to seek redress in a cause amounts to
laches and the judgment will not be disturbed, especially
where the
status
quo
cannot be restored.
These error proceedings grew out of an action of damages in the Civil Law
Court for the Sixth Judicial Circuit, Montserrado County.
The case is before the
Supreme Court for the second time. When the case was before the court for the
first time, the co-defendant-in-error,
then plaintiff-in-error, contended that
the court below did not acquire jurisdiction of the defendant company by means
of service
of a writ of summons to appear and defend the action, and therefore,
could not be concluded by a judgment of a court which had not
acquired
jurisdiction over it. The Supreme Court heard the proceeding and reversed the
judgment of the lower court for improper service
of summons, with instruction
for the parties to replead, commencing with the complaint.
Plaintiff refiled this second action claiming damages for breach of contract
as directed by the Supreme Court's mandate in the first
action. The defendant
company filed an answer, simultaneously with a motion to dismiss the complaint,
on the ground of laches, contending
that the second action of damages should
have been filed within six (6) months from the date of the Supreme Court
opinion, but that
plaintiff elected to refile his action after eight (8) months,
more than the time allowed by statute and case law. Plaintiff filed
a resistance
to the motion to dismiss and contended therein that the Supreme Court's opinion
did not set any time limit within which
to refile the action and therefore, the
time within which the action was refiled was reasonable. It is from the ruling
granting the
motion to dismiss that the second writ of error has been applied
for and issued.
The Supreme Court, upon review of the records, held that error will not lie,
in that the plaintiff had its day in court, but neglected
to take advantage of
its rights when it was duly notified by a notice of assignment to appear and
argue its resistance. The Court
also held that the repleading mandated by the
Supreme Court is governed by the Civil Procedure Law, which provides that the
plaintiff
reinstitute his action in six months and that the failure to do so
constitutes a waiver. Accordingly, the judgment of the lower court
was affirmed.
Eugene D. Freeman
appeared for the plaintiff-in-error.
Pearl
Brown-Bull
appeared for the defendants-in-error.
MR. JUSTICE SMITH delivered the opinion of the Court.
This is the second
time that this case has come up to this Court on a writ of error for our review.
The first time was in 1993 during
the October Term of this Court. At that time,
the co-defendant-in-error, then the plaintiff-in-error, contended that the Court
below
did not acquire jurisdiction over the defendant company by means of
service of a writ of summons to appear and defend the action,
and therefore, it
could not be concluded by a judgment of a court which had not acquired
jurisdiction over it. This Court heard the
petition and reversed the judgment of
the lower court for improper service of summons on the proper corporate or
managerial officer
of the defendant company or on the registered agent of the
company, with instruction for the parties to replead commencing with the
complaint. The opinion of the Court was delivered on February 18, 1994, at the
close of the October Term, A. D. 1993.
On November 8, 1994, that is to say, eight (8) months and twenty-two (22)
days after the opinion of the Supreme Court, A. Hejazi Corporation,
plaintiff in
the action of damages for breach of contract, re-filed this second action
claiming damages for breach of contract as
directed by the Supreme Court's
mandate in the first action. The defendant company filed an answer
simultaneously with a motion to
dismiss the complaint on the ground of laches,
contending that the second action of damages should have been filed as
instructed
by the Supreme Court within six (6) months from the date of the
Supreme Court's opinion, delivered on February 18, 1994, and not
after eight (8)
months, more than the time allowed by statute and case law. Plaintiff filed a
resistance to the motion to dismiss
and contended therein that the Supreme
Court's opinion did not set any time limit within which to refile the action,
and therefore,
the time within which the action was re-filed was reasonable.
The motion to dismiss was assigned for hearing on January 6, 1995. Notice of
assignment was accordingly served and Attorney James
E. Jones of the Toye C.
Barnard Law Firm, acknowledged the assignment and made a footnote on the
assignment to the effect that Counsellor
Barnard, the leading counsel of record
in the case was sick and had been taken to the United States of America for
medical treatment.
In the footnote, the attorney went on to say: "We therefore
request that the case be postponed pending his return or his instruction."
Recourse to the case file, however, shows that the motion to dismiss was
resisted by counsel for plaintiff which said resistance
was signed by Counsellor
Barnard and the said attorney James Jones. Therefore, one of the counsels for
plaintiff was in the country
and could have appeared to argue the resistance.
Sickness of a counsel for a party or his material witness is a good ground
for postponement or continuance of a cause, but the court
must be timely
notified by a motion for continuance, with notice to the opposite party, and
such motion must be heard in open court
with the movant present to argue.
However, where there is more than one lawyer in a case, the sickness of one
lawyer will not excuse
the other who is available and has signed the notice of
assignment, as in this case. In the instant case, even though the leading
counsel was ill and out of the country for medical treatment, there was no
excuse for Attorney Jones who signed the notice of assignment
as well as the
resistance to the motion, not to have appeared for argument. The Circuit Court
Rules provide that a party who is not
ready for trial because of illness or
absence of a material witness, or for some other legal reasons, may file a
motion for continuance,
and where he is available, he must appear in court and
argue his motion for continuance, a copy of which must have been served on
the
opposite party under the principle of notice. It is not the practice in this
jurisdiction for a party to write an excuse on the
notice of assignment, as did
attorney Jones of the Toye C. Barnard Law Firm, and then stay away from court
and expect the court to
assume the role of counsel for the plaintiff. When the
motion was called for disposition, Attorney Jones, one of counsels for the
plaintiff who signed the notice of assignment did not appear to show cause why
the motion should not be argued in the absence of
Counsellor Barnard. Hence, the
court proceeded to entertain argument of the counsel for defendant/ movant and
to rule granting the
motion to dismiss. It is from this ruling that the second
writ of error has been applied for and issued.
Of the several issues argued by both counsels and presented in their briefs,
there are only two (2) issues which, in our opinion,
are important to the fair
determination of this case. We are not inclined to discuss the other issues
which, in our opinion, are
not decisive of the controversy. The issues which we
deem necessary and decisive of this proceeding are:
(1) Whether or not a writ of error will issue from the circumstances
attending this case, and
(2) whether or not the plaintiff is guilty of laches and waiver, not having
filed its complaint as directed by the Supreme Court within
the period of six
(6) months from the date of the opinion, i.e., February 18, 1994.
A writ of error is defined by our statute as a writ by which the Supreme
Court calls up for review a judgment of an inferior court
from which an appeal
was not announced on rendition of judgment. Civil Procedure Law, Rev. Code 1:
16.21 (4). The statute also provides
that a party against whom judgment has been
taken, who has for good reason failed to make timely announcement of the taking
of an
appeal from such judgment, may, within six (6) months after its rendition
file with the Clerk of the Supreme Court an application
for leave for a review
by the Supreme Court upon a writ of error.
Id.,
1:16.24. What would be
good reason given by a party who has been afforded the opportunity to be present
in court at the rendition
ofjudgment? This Court has held in several opinions
that a party who was not afforded the opportunity to have his day in court by
the failure of the court to notify him of the time and place of trial, or of the
rendition ofjudgment, and therefore did not know
that a judgment was to be
rendered against him to afford him the opportunity to except and announce
appeal, would be a good reason.
In the case
Kurah et al v. Geeby
[1960] LRSC 50
; ,
14 LLR
147
(1960), this Court said that the good reason allegation is not conclusive,
but the plaintiff-in-error must establish and the court
must be convinced, that
the reason alleged is good in law. This Court also held that a writ of error may
be granted when an inferior
tribunal has denied to a litigant his day in court.
Where the appellant, as held by this Court, has lost his right of statutory
appeal
without laches, or where he is incompetent to act, or not notified of the
rendition of the decision until the statutory time for
the taking of an appeal
has elapsed, he may resort to a writ of error.
Wodawodey v. Kartiehn,
4
LLR 102
(1934). "A day in court" as defined by BLACKS LAW DIC-TIONARY 474 (4'
ed) is the time appointed for one whose rights are called judiciously
in
question, or liable to be affected by judicial action, to appear in court and be
heard in his own behalf. This phrase, as generally
used, means not so much the
time appointed for a hearing, as the opportunity to present one's claims or
rights in a proper forensic
hearing before a competent tribunal. A litigant has
his "day in court" when he has been duly cited to appear and has been afforded
the opportunity to appear and be heard.
Grass Roots Cinema et al. v. Pupo and
Citibank NA.,
[1984] LRSC 57
;
32 LLR 478
(1984), October Term, 1984. In the case at bar,
defendant' motion to dismiss the action was assigned for argument on January 25,
1995, at the hour of 12:00 noon. This assignment was acknowledged by Attorney
James Jones, one of counsel for plaintiff in the Toye
C. Barnard Law Firm, but
he neither appeared to argue nor filed a motion for continuance, as provided by
law in such cases where,
for legal reasons, the party is not ready for trial.
The co-respondent judge therefore afforded the defendant who appeared in court
an opportunity, as the law directs, to argue its side of the motion. It cannot
therefore be said that the plaintiff did not have
its day in court, having
neglected to take advantage of its rights when it was duly notified by a notice
of assignment to appear
and argue its resistance. Error will therefore not lie
and cannot be used to benefit the plaintiff for its own negligence.
We now come to the second and last of the only two (2) issues we deem
necessary, and which, in our opinion, is decisive of this case,
that is, the
refiling of the plaintiff's case as directed by the Supreme Court, in its
February 18, 1994 opinion. Plaintiff-in-error
filed its complaint eight (8)
months and twenty-two (22) days after the aforesaid opinion. Plaintiff-in-error
contends that the eight
(8) month period was reasonable, as the Supreme Court's
mandate did not set any time frame within which to replead. This argument
of the
plaintiff-in-error's counsel is untenable and we quote, word for word, our
statute law on the point:
"If an action is timely commenced and is
terminated in any manner other than by a dismissal of the complaint for failure
to prosecute
the action or a final judgment on the merits, the plaintiff may
commence a new action upon the same right to relief after the expiration
of the
time limited by statute therefor and within six (6) months after the
termination, and the defendant may interpose any defense
or counter claim which
might have been interposed in the original action." Civil Procedure Law, Rev.
Code 1:2.73.
The action of damages for breach of contract was neither dismissed by the
trial court nor by this Court. It was terminated, that is,
put out of court,
because the writ of summons was not properly served on the defendant company as
the law directs. Only the complaint
was in court, to which no answer was filed.
Hence the Supreme Court, in the interest of justice, reversed the lower court's
judgment
in the first action and remanded the case for the parties to replead,
which is the same as filing the action anew to permit the issuance
and service
of a new writ of summons for service on the proper officer of the defendant
company, thereby terminating the first action.
The statute quoted above demands
that an action which has been timely commenced, may be terminated by any manner
other than dismissal
and, that within six (6) months after the termination, the
same may be refiled and the defendant may interpose any defense, to even
include
a counter claim, which might have been interposed in the original action. It is
our opinion, therefore, that after the plaintiff-in-error
company had been
afforded the opportunity to replead, and not just a reversal of the judgment, it
was incumbent upon it to have taken
advantage of its right at the proper time.
Not having repleaded by filing its complaint as directed by this Court and
within the
time allowed by statute, plaintiff-in-error cannot claim that the
mandate of the Supreme Court did not set any time limit within
which to refile
the action or replead. The statute has already prescribed the time in such cases
with which to file a new action.
Plaintiff-in-error claims that eight (8) months and twenty two (22) days
after the Supreme Court opinion was a reasonable time, as
contemplated by law,
and it cited in its brief and strongly argued that in the case
Cooper-King v.
Cooper-Scott,
[1963] LRSC 38
;
15
LLR 390
(1963), the Supreme Court held that the rules which
apply to withdrawal of pleadings and the amendment of pleadings before pleadings
are rested, do not control the mandates of the Supreme Court which command the
parties to replead on remand, and that such orders
are to be executed strictly
as they are given. There is nothing wrong with the holding of the Court in that
case, for it correctly
settled the issues that were raised therein. In the said
case, one of the issues was the withdrawal of the complaint by plaintiff
after
the remand with, instructions to replead, when indeed there was no complaint in
court to amend. In that case, the pleadings
did not clearly and concisely
present the issues and so the Supreme Court remanded the case for the parties to
replead. The action
was not terminated because the writ of summons which brought
the parties under the jurisdiction of the court was still in court and
was never
attacked. But instead of the plaintiff re-pleading by filing a complaint and
presenting the issues clearly and concisely
as was ordered by the Supreme Court,
the plaintiff filed a notice of withdrawal and an amended complaint when indeed
there was no
complaint in court to amend. This was contrary to the instructions
given by the Court to replead. This is why the Supreme Court in
that case
distinguished the rules controlling withdrawal and amendment of pleadings, and
amendment of pleadings from mandates of
the Supreme Court, containing orders
which command the parties to replead or remand, which orders must be executed as
strictly as
they are given.
The facts in the
Cooper-King v. Cooper-Scott
case and those of the
instant case are therefore not analogous. In this case, the writ of summons was
not properly served and returned
served to place the defendant company under the
jurisdiction of the court, which would have enabled the defendant to file an
answer.
There was only a complaint in court. Hence it was thrown out, and the
court thereupon remanded the case with instructions to replead
in the interest
of justice, which would have enabled plaintiff to file a new complaint and the
court to issue a new writ of summons,
to be served on the proper officer of the
defendant company as the law directs. Under the circumstances, not only was the
first action
terminated, but the writ of summons was no longer in court. The
procedure to bring back to court a terminated case is what our statute
prescribes in §2.73 of the Civil Procedure Law, Revised Code 1, quoted
supra,
that after a case has been terminated and the Supreme Court orders
the parties to replead, the plaintiff must commence his new action
within six
(6) months. In the first error proceeding, the Supreme Court heard the case and
granted said writ in an opinion delivered
on February 18, 1994, at the close of
the October Term, A. D. 1993. After a period of eight (8) months and twenty-two
(22) days,
that is to say, on November 8, 1994, plaintiff filed his complaint,
thereby commencing the pleadings in keeping with the instruction
of the Supreme
Court, but without the time allowed by statute to do so. The defendant company,
therefore, moved for dismissal of
the second action on the ground of laches, and
the court ruled granting the motion and dismissing the second action. Plaintiff
should
have filed his new complaint within six (6) months as the statute
commands, but it failed and neglected to do so until a period of
eight (8)
months—almost nine (9) months—before bringing back the terminated
case contrary to the command of the statute.
We hold, therefore, that the trial
court did not err when it granted the motion to dismiss. We are not however in
agreement, if the
granting of the motion was based only on the law of default on
motion, because the motion to dismiss was opposed by the plaintiff,
by the
filing of a resistance thereto, which was in court, even though the resistance
cannot override the question of laches and
waiver, which plaintiff must indeed
suffer for failure to comply with the statute law.
Chapters 21 and 22 of the Civil Procedure Law, Rev. Code 1, which provide for
trial procedure contain no provisions which mandatorily
require a trial judge
before whom a motion is made, not to rule on the motion at the close of
argument, and that doing so would be
a ground for reversal. To reserve ruling on
a motion at the close of argument or to rule immediately thereafter is left with
the
discretion of the trial judge and his legal ability to pass on the issues
raised. The law only provides that upon the return of a
verdict in a jury trial,
to which exceptions are noted, four (4) days must be allowed for the filing of a
motion for new trial; and
after ruling on the motion for new trial, the judge
may immediately render final judgment, if the motion is denied. In a criminal
trial, five (5) days are allowed after a verdict to permit the filing of a
motion in arrest of judgment. But no time is required
to elapse before a judge
can pass on a motion argued before him. The motion to dismiss was filed
simultaneously with the defendant's
answer, and, in the disposition of the legal
issues raised in the pleadings, the motion raising the legal issues of laches
and waiver
had to be disposed of first. To ensure promptness and punctuality,
Rule 28 of the Circuit Court Rules provides, as follows:
"The clerk shall enter upon the ordinary docket of the court all matters
filed in his office, and whether the pleadings are concluded,
and issues joined
. . . , he shall notify the judge thereof, who shall assign a day for passing
upon the issues of law and hearing
all cases not dismissed on questions of law,
whether or not the counsels previously notified are present. All cases which are
proper
to be tried by jury shall be transferred to the trial docket. The clerk
of court shall, five (5) days before the meeting of the trial
session, make out
the trial calendar and furnish the judge a copy thereof." Plaintiff-in-error,
having been afforded an opportunity
to appear in court by means of a notice of
assignment informing the parties of the time and place of hearing, and counsel
for plaintiff-in-error
having received and signed the notice, and failed and
neglected to appear, it cannot claim that it did not have its day in court.
In
Monrovia Breweries Inc. v. Hilton and Tarplah,
37 LLR 409
(1993),
October Term, 1993, this Court held, with respect to bringing back to court an
action which has been terminated, as in this
case, that:
"The ruling of the lower court dismissing the case filed by appellee was
given on July 3, 1982. The complaint for the new action was
filed by the
appellee on March 3, 1983, eight months later. Section 2.73 of the Civil
Procedure Law permits a party up to six months
after termination to substitute a
new action, given the facts and circumstances of the present case." Mr. Justice
Hne, who spoke
for this Court in that case, went on to say:
"The appellee argues, however, that termination takes place after the
expiration of the sixty days allowed him by statute to complete
his appeal. Our
reading of the statute is clear to us that termination takes place on the date
of the Court's ruling terminating
the case. The appellee was therefore outside
the period provided in section 2.73 when he filed the new motion. The expiration
of
six months puts finality to the case."
Id.,
pp. 65.
Every man is entitled to take full advantage of the law in defense of his
right; but if he fails to do so, the law gives no protection
to him for abusing
his own rights. It has also been held that he who is silent when he should
speak, assents.
Pongoy v. Obey and Korlubah,
[1982] LRSC 9
;
29 LLR 500
(1981), October
Term;
Clark et al. v. Lewis
[1929] LRSC 5
; ,
3 LLR 95
(1929);
Vietor & Huber v.
Thatcher,
[1912] LRSC 6
;
2 LLR 80
(1912).
The unreasonable delay of a party to seek redress in a cause amounts to
laches, and in such a case the judgment will not be disturbed,
especially where
the
status quo
cannot be restored. According to public policy, there must
be an end to litigation. It has been held that to require courts to consider
and
reconsider cases at the will of litigants, would deprive the courts of that
stability which is necessary for the proper administration
of justice.
In view of the foregoing, and the citation of law controlling, it is our
considered opinion that the ruling of the trial court granting
the motion to
dismiss should not be disturbed. And it is hereby so ordered.
Petition denied.
